speaker: interviewer
text: 1996 and we're in Sydney, Australia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, we're talking about your family and how they helped you with your war experiences. You told them about it. What was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell them so much about it. I admit that they knew that obviously, I was in the Army. They knew that I was abroad. They knew I'd gone through all these countries. And I think, like my wife, God rest her, she goes, thank God you'd come back safe and well, I didn't with my other family who were in the services.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you tell them about your experiences and what you saw at Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell them about what you saw at Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I mentioned it a couple of times, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your later civilian life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose all I could say is that, please, God, this should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it shouldn't have happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: By it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course, I am. Of course, I'm affected by it. I mean, I only have to see a film or some photo of the thing and I get very emotional about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Has your experiences affected any of the life choices that you've made?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way, really?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, are you still a communist?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I'm ardently anti-fascist. I'm anti-anti-Semitism. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm anti [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] societies that could create this kind of thing. So I hope that it never has to happen. Although, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unfortunately, I think something we can never get rid of, anti-Semitism. It's something I don't think we can ever get rid of. Doesn't matter how hard we try, doesn't matter how many people you tell there was  a Holocaust, doesn't matter how many people you tell six million Jews died in this savage way, I don't think you could ever eradicate it, but to me, which is [BACKROUND NOISE] one of the great tragedies in the world today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you say to those people who deny the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I've got proof. I've seen it. What else can I say? Because they never ask the people who've experienced it. They only ask the people about the Holocaust who have never experienced the war, experienced the pictures we've seen and things like that. So they've always got a-- an audience there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, you've told us you've kept in touch with the Esse family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still in touch with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. The last time I got a-- my wife got a letter from them was 1945. I've got the letters here. I mean, I've got some letters here written by the Esse family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have been back to Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went in 1963 with your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, the place was completely unrecognizable. It's been cleared up. The tombstones there or the graves, the mass graves that are there had all been ornamented, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bricked up and with plaques on it, how many people died and all that. And there's a memorial there, which is a very-- quite a moving experience. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, obviously, it's not the plaques what I saw. What I-- yeah, entirely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it bring back floods of memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah, when I went there. Of course, it did. Yeah, naturally, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say to your children or grandchildren that you haven't already said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've always told them to try to be good Jewish people, not to create hatred or jealousy. Don't be ostentatious in your ways. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And do good, just don't do anything bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you might like to say to future generations of human beings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, learn what hatred could do. Remember what hatred could do. Remember all the atrocities that human beings could do on other people. What else could I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you want to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I think that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks for sharing this with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure, Susie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it does some good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this photo, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is of my late mother and father, God rest their souls. This photo was taken prior to 1939. My father's name was Herschl. My mother's name was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Finer, F-I-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of me and my twin brother Jack taken at Settles Street School, Whitechapel, London, E1. The second row from the front, I am on the right side of my brother in a white shirt, and my twin brother's on the left side of me in a white shirt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish school or a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a mixed school. My headmaster's name was Mr. Lewis and my teacher's name was Mr. Rosenwine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. When he tells us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of me taken in the-- when I was in the Middlesex Regiment, 1939. This was a machine gun regiment, and this is the first photo I took after I enlisted, which would be around about April of 1939. All the flags?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the flags are flags of the British Empire. This photo was taken at Mill Hill Barracks, Battersea Hill, Hendon, London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of myself and my late wife, Ann, taken in 1938 on our engagement. Photo was taken in London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of myself taken whilst I was on R and R leave in Belgium. The photo was taken in the city of Ghent, G-H-E-N-T, normally spelled G-I-N-D. This was taken round about June, July-- August '44. I think August '44 anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a photo of my late wife, Ann, with my eldest son, David, on the left-hand side of the picture and Gloria on the right side of the picture. David was born 1940. Gloria was born 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in London and sent to me whilst I was in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And wait for it, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my younger son Barry. He was born 24th September 1954. The film was taken-- or the photo was taken in Romford, Essex.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate of my service during my term with the Regular Army, which as you can see shows my name, Army number, and where I was born, and enlisted, which was 22nd of March 1939. I enlisted with the Middlesex Regiment and machine gun regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a record of my service with the British Army from the 22nd of March 1939 to September '46. Also shows my-- on the other page-- my tour of duties whilst I was with the Army, whilst I was with the Army anyway. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a recent photograph of myself taken 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Sydney-- Sydney, Australia. I was gonna say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape four of the interview with Michael Sheridan. It's the 15th  of April 1996. We're in Sydney, Australia, and that's also the end of the whole interview.
